hypotheses: Hypothesis 1: In one-stage tournaments, effort levels of participants will align with the theoretical predictions based on Nash equilibrium.
Hypothesis 2: In two-stage tournaments, initial effort levels will exceed equilibrium predictions due to forward-looking behavior and overestimation of future promotion values.
claim.statement: In two-stage tournaments, initial effort levels will exceed theoretical predictions due to forward-looking behavior and overestimation of future promotion values.

data.notes: Experiment conducted using z-Tree software and ORSEE for recruitment.

method.description: The study used controlled laboratory experiments to compare one-stage and two-stage tournament models.
steps: Participants were split into one-stage and two-stage tournament treatments.
Effort levels were measured in both settings.
The Nash equilibrium was used as a theoretical benchmark for effort levels.
Statistical tests were conducted to compare effort levels between treatments.

results.summary: Participants in two-stage tournaments exerted higher initial effort than predicted by equilibrium models, while one-stage tournament efforts aligned more closely with predictions.

numerical_results.outcome_name: First Stage Effort in Two-Stage Tournament

metadata.original_paper_title: Incentives in Multistage Elimination Tournaments: Experimental Evidence
